# Geometry & Masks

Three rasterization primitives — `rect_mask`, `disk_mask`, `polygon_mask` —
turn shape descriptions into 0/1 indicator matrices on a `meshgrid`. They
exist to keep geometry-building code (which is mostly bookkeeping) out of
the way of the physics or signal-processing problem you actually care
about. Each builder returns an `ny × nx` real-valued matrix the same shape
as the meshgrid inputs, with `1.0` inside the shape and `0.0` outside.

The output is binary, not anti-aliased. Composition is just element-wise
math, which means you can build complicated geometry — multi-region
material maps, layered devices, doped regions inside doped regions — using
nothing more than `.*`, `+`, and `1 - M`.

## A grid to draw on

Every mask consumes coordinate matrices `X` and `Y` from `meshgrid`, so
that's the first thing to set up:

```rustlab
clf
N = 200;
[X, Y] = meshgrid(linspace(-1.5, 1.5, N), linspace(-1.5, 1.5, N));
print(size(X))     % → [200, 200]   rows = y, cols = x
```

```text
[1×2]  200.000000  200.000000
```

The shape convention is the same as `gradient`, `imagesc`, and `contour`:
rows index `y`, columns index `x`. Masks return matrices of the same
shape, so they drop straight into anything that consumes a meshgrid.

## `rect_mask(X, Y, x0, y0, w, h)`

Axis-aligned rectangle, **inclusive on all four sides**:

$$M(i,j) = 1 \iff x_0 \le X(i,j) \le x_0+w \\,\text{ and }\\, y_0 \le Y(i,j) \le y_0+h$$

`(x0, y0)` is the lower-left corner; `w` and `h` must be finite and
non-negative. Zero-extent rectangles are allowed and match only the line
or point at the boundary.

```rustlab
clf
R = rect_mask(X, Y, -0.8, -0.4, 1.2, 0.8);
imagesc(R);
title("rect_mask: lower-left (-0.8, -0.4), 1.2 × 0.8")
```

![plot 1](plots/masks/plot-1.svg)

## `disk_mask(X, Y, xc, yc, r)`

Closed disk:

$$M(i,j) = 1 \iff (X(i,j) - x_c)^2 + (Y(i,j) - y_c)^2 \le r^2$$

`r` must be finite and non-negative; `r = 0` matches only the cells
closest to the centre.

```rustlab
clf
D = disk_mask(X, Y, 0.0, 0.0, 0.9);
imagesc(D);
title("disk_mask: centre (0, 0), radius 0.9")
```

![plot 2](plots/masks/plot-2.svg)

### Numerical sanity: integrating the mask gives π·r²

A unit disk on this grid, integrated against the cell area, should
approximate $\pi r^2 = \pi$. Closeness depends on grid resolution and on
the closed-vs-open boundary convention — `disk_mask` includes the
boundary, so the estimate slightly overshoots:

```rustlab
unit_disk = disk_mask(X, Y, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0);
step = 3.0 / (N - 1);
print(sum(sum(unit_disk)) * step * step)    % → ~3.135 (0.2 % off π)
```

```text
3.1353753693088553
```

## `polygon_mask(X, Y, verts)`

Polygon defined by an `N × 2` matrix of vertices, each row `[x, y]`. The
polygon is implicitly closed — an edge runs from vertex `N-1` back to
vertex `0`. The implementation uses **even-odd ray casting** (PNPOLY):
shoot a horizontal ray from the test point and count how many polygon
edges it crosses; an odd count means inside.

```rustlab
clf
T = polygon_mask(X, Y, [-1.0,-1.0; 1.0,-1.0; 0.0,1.0]);
imagesc(T);
title("polygon_mask: triangle (-1,-1)-(1,-1)-(0,1)")
```

![plot 3](plots/masks/plot-3.svg)

Degenerate inputs return an all-zero mask without panicking:

| Input                                   | Result        |
|-----------------------------------------|---------------|
| Fewer than 3 vertices                   | All zero      |
| All vertices collinear (zero area)      | All zero      |
| `verts` not shape `N × 2`               | Error         |

Behaviour at points exactly on a polygon edge is implementation-defined.
PNPOLY's strict inequality returns a deterministic 0 or 1 but it is not
guaranteed to match any particular convention on the boundary; if you
need exact-edge semantics, perturb the polygon slightly.

## Boolean composition is just element-wise math

Because the masks are 0/1 valued, set operations are arithmetic:

| Operation                              | Expression                  |
|----------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Intersection $M_1 \cap M_2$            | `M1 .* M2`                  |
| Complement $\overline{M}$              | `1 - M`                     |
| Union $M_1 \cup M_2$                   | `M1 + M2 - M1 .* M2`        |
| Set difference $M_1 \setminus M_2$     | `M1 .* (1 - M2)`            |

Note that `max(M1, M2)` does **not** work for the union — `max` in
rustlab takes a scalar argument. The inclusion-exclusion form
`M1 + M2 - M1 .* M2` is the right idiom for binary masks.

To visualise multiple regions in a single image, encode each region as a
distinct integer:

```rustlab
clf
both   = R .* D;
r_only = R .* (1 - D);
d_only = D .* (1 - R);

% 0 = outside both, 1 = R only, 2 = D only, 3 = R ∩ D
regions = 1 * r_only + 2 * d_only + 3 * both;
imagesc(regions);
title("Two-shape regions: R only / D only / overlap")
```

![plot 4](plots/masks/plot-4.svg)

## Building a layered material map

The canonical use case for the masks is a multi-region material map —
say, a substrate with a circular via and a rectangular contact pad on
top. Each layer **overwrites** any earlier layer where they overlap, via
the `device .* (1 - layer) + id * layer` idiom:

```rustlab
clf
substrate = rect_mask(X, Y, -1.2, -0.6, 2.4, 1.2);
via       = disk_mask(X, Y, -0.4, 0.0, 0.25);
contact   = polygon_mask(X, Y, [0.2,-0.3; 0.9,-0.3; 0.9,0.3; 0.2,0.3]);
trace     = rect_mask(X, Y, 0.2, -0.05, 0.7, 0.1);

device =        1 * substrate;                          % material 1
device = device .* (1 - via)     + 2 * via;             % material 2 over 1
device = device .* (1 - contact) + 3 * contact;         % material 3 over 1, 2
device = device .* (1 - trace)   + 4 * trace;           % material 4 over 1, 2, 3

imagesc(device);
title("Layered device: substrate / via / contact / trace")
```

![plot 5](plots/masks/plot-5.svg)

The result is a single `ny × nx` matrix where the value of every cell is
the integer ID of the topmost layer at that point — exactly the format a
PDE solver wants when it indexes a per-material lookup of $\varepsilon_r$,
conductivity, or any other property.

## Why masks are 0/1 and not anti-aliased

Two reasons. First, almost every downstream use is logical: indexing
into per-material arrays, gating boundary conditions, deciding whether
a cell contributes to a sum. Anti-aliased masks would force every
consumer to reason about partial-cell semantics. Second, when partial
coverage *does* matter (e.g. computing the area of a curved region near
the grid resolution), the right fix is **conformal area weighting** on
top of a binary mask, not blurring the mask. Keeping the primitives
binary keeps the composition rules clean.

## Cheat sheet

| Form                                              | Returns                          | Notes                                    |
|---------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| `rect_mask(X, Y, x0, y0, w, h)`                   | `Matrix` same shape as `X`/`Y`   | Inclusive on all four sides              |
| `disk_mask(X, Y, xc, yc, r)`                      | `Matrix` same shape as `X`/`Y`   | Closed (boundary included); `r ≥ 0`      |
| `polygon_mask(X, Y, verts)`                       | `Matrix` same shape as `X`/`Y`   | `verts` is `N × 2`; PNPOLY ray casting   |
| `M1 .* M2`                                        | intersection                     |                                          |
| `1 - M`                                           | complement                       |                                          |
| `M1 + M2 - M1 .* M2`                              | union                            | Don't use `max` — it takes scalars only  |
| `M1 .* (1 - M2)`                                  | set difference $M_1 \setminus M_2$ |                                        |
| `device .* (1 - layer) + id * layer`              | layered material map             | Each layer overwrites earlier ones       |

Each builder validates its inputs: shape mismatches between `X` and `Y`,
non-`N × 2` `verts`, and non-finite or negative dimensions all return a
clean error rather than producing a corrupt mask.
